assumptions: Traffic ~<1k–5k concurrent sessions/mo today; sized accordingly. Growth axis is pipeline throughput + cached content volume, not concurrent dynamic requests.
Single US region throughout; multi-region DR deferred to resilient tier.
No PCI/HIPAA/regulated data — standard baseline security floor applied.
ISR cache is in-process (Next.js file-system / memory) on the Node container; no external cache store required at budget tier.
Orchestrator job queue uses pg-boss or similar Postgres-backed queue library — Postgres already present, no separate queue service at budget.
Headless Chromium render function requires >512 MB RAM; sized at 2048 MB Lambda. Output >6 MB written to S3 and returned by reference per brief.
Nightly backup = pg_dump via EventBridge-scheduled Lambda → S3 (Glacier IR). Reconciliation cron = separate scheduled Lambda.
Schema migrations run as a CodeBuild/ECS one-off task triggered at deploy; not a standing service.
Balanced tier introduces private subnet + NAT gateway for RDS; cost delta noted.
Resilient tier adds multi-AZ RDS, ALB, Fargate multi-task, and cross-region S3 replication for DR-grade availability.
Instance sizing: budget single-box t4g.small (2 vCPU / 2 GB) is tight for Node + Postgres + orchestrator; t4g.medium (2 vCPU / 4 GB) matches current host footprint and is used instead.
Cost estimates are on-demand list prices for us-east-1, presented as ranges over assumed monthly volumes. They exclude the AWS Free Tier, Savings Plans, Reserved Instances, and negotiated discounts.
Assumed traffic ~100k–1M requests/month, applied equally to all three tiers (tiers differ by robustness, not traffic). Set "expected monthly visitors" in intake to change it.
clarificationsUsed: Cost-optimized / single-AZ acceptable → budget tier is a single EC2 box (Docker Compose: web + orchestrator + Postgres/PostGIS) with render Lambda split out.
Render output can exceed 6 MB → Lambda writes large output to S3 and returns presigned URL.
Orchestrator is Postgres-backed job queue → reuses existing Postgres instance; no SQS/separate broker at budget.
Traffic very low today → t4g.medium single box covers current 2 vCPU/4 GB footprint; no ALB or NAT at budget.
PostGIS required → self-managed Postgres + PostGIS extension on budget box; RDS for PostgreSQL with PostGIS at balanced+.
Nightly backup + 14-day retention → pg_dump Lambda → S3 lifecycle (Standard → Glacier IR at day 1, expire day 14).

tiers.name: budget
tiers.summary: Single EC2 t4g.medium (Docker Compose: Next.js web + orchestrator + self-managed Postgres/PostGIS) behind CloudFront+WAF. Render Lambda split out per brief. Nightly pg_dump and cron jobs via scheduled Lambda. ~$0 idle on Lambda; ~$30/mo floor for EC2 + S3.

security: restrictive SG (443/80 from CF only, no direct public DB port)
lifecycle: Glacier IR day 1, expire day 14

delta: Baseline: single EC2 t4g.medium (Docker Compose) — web + orchestrator + self-managed Postgres/PostGIS co-located, ~$30/mo floor
Render Lambda split out immediately per brief (scale-to-zero, pay-per-render)
No ALB, no NAT gateway, no RDS — public subnet with tight SG; self-managed Postgres on encrypted EBS (localhost-only)
Postgres-backed job queue (pg-boss) reuses existing DB; no SQS/broker
ISR cache: in-process Next.js file-system on container ephemeral storage; S3 as revalidation write-through
Burst handling: render Lambda reserved concurrency cap; CloudFront caches ISR pages; API Gateway throttling not applicable (direct CF→EC2)
Observability: CloudWatch Logs + key alarms (5xx rate, Lambda errors, EC2 CPU) → SNS email; X-Ray tracing on web + render paths
tradeoffs: Single-AZ: any EC2/EBS failure = downtime until instance recovery (RPO/RTO relaxed per brief)
Self-managed Postgres: no automated failover, no read replicas, manual patching
Co-located services: noisy-neighbor risk between web, orchestrator, and DB on one box
No connection pooler (PgBouncer) — add if Lambda/cron connection churn becomes an issue
Lambda cold starts for render function (mitigated by arm64 + Layer caching)

costDrivers.note: Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier (North America). First 1TB/month is free.
HTTPS requests ($0.0075 per 10,000). North America/Europe.
S3 Standard storage, first 50TB/month tier.
PUT/COPY/POST/LIST requests per 1,000. GET/SELECT requests are ~$0.0004 per 1,000.
Assumed-throughput estimate: t4g.medium on-demand (us-east-1). Cost = instance count × running hours; storage/IO billed separately, multi-AZ via the tier multiplier.
Compute duration: $0.0000166667 per GB-second (x86). Multiply by memoryGB x durationSeconds per invocation.
On-demand list price ($0.20 per 1M requests). Excludes Free Tier.
Custom event-bus events: $1.00 per 1M events published. Pipes/schema registry priced separately.
CloudWatch Logs ingestion $0.50/GB (first 10GB/mo free in some accounts). Archival storage is cheaper; Insights queries extra.
Standard topic API requests ($0.50 per 1M). Delivery (HTTP/SMS/email) priced separately.
X-Ray traces: $5.00 per 1M traces recorded (first 100k/mo free).

tiers.name: balanced
tiers.summary: Promote to managed split: Fargate (web + orchestrator as separate services) + RDS for PostgreSQL with PostGIS + private subnets + NAT Gateway + ALB. S3-backed ISR write-through. ElastiCache Redis for ISR/session cache. Render Lambda unchanged. Multi-AZ optional (not default — single-AZ RDS at this tier).

security: lifecycle: Glacier IR day 1, expire day 14

delta: EC2 single-box → Fargate: web and orchestrator become independent ECS services (scale independently per brief)
Self-managed Postgres → RDS for PostgreSQL (db.t4g.medium) with PostGIS extension; automated backups + 14d retention
Private subnets introduced for Fargate tasks + RDS + ElastiCache → NAT Gateway required for outbound egress (~$33/mo always-on)
ALB added as HTTPS ingress in front of Fargate tasks
ElastiCache Redis (cache.t4g.micro) for shared ISR + session cache across tasks
S3-backed ISR write-through replaces ephemeral in-process cache (consistent across tasks)
CloudWatch Dashboard added for golden-signal visibility
Render Lambda: unchanged — still scale-to-zero
Burst handling: Fargate service auto-scaling (CPU/request-count); ElastiCache absorbs hot-read bursts; CloudFront ISR caching unchanged
tradeoffs: ~$100–130/mo always-on floor (NAT + ALB + RDS + ElastiCache) vs. ~$30/mo budget
Still single-AZ RDS and single NAT — AZ failure = DB downtime; multi-AZ promoted to resilient
NAT Gateway egress cost scales with AI API call volume — monitor outbound data transfer
Fargate cold-start latency on scale-out events (mitigated by minimum task count of 1 per service)

Application Load Balancer per-hour charge (always-on).

Per GB processed. Forced by the private-subnet data-tier default (security baseline no-public-data-tier); the most common budget surprise.
Per NAT gateway-hour (always-on, ~$32.40/month per AZ before data processing).
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Inter-AZ data transfer, charged $0.01/GB in each direction.
Required by the private-subnet security default (no public data tier) — the most common budget surprise. Data transfer out to internet, first 10TB/month tier. First 100GB/month is free.

tiers.name: resilient
tiers.summary: Multi-AZ everywhere: RDS Multi-AZ standby, ALB + Fargate across 2 AZs, NAT per AZ, ElastiCache Multi-AZ. EventBridge + SQS fan-out replaces direct Lambda invoke for orchestrator. CloudFront + S3 cross-region replication for DR. SLO alarms + PagerDuty escalation.

security: lifecycle: Glacier IR day 1, expire day 14
S3 CRR from primary backup bucket

edges.payload: Pipeline job event (city ID, job type)

delta: RDS promoted to Multi-AZ standby (automated synchronous failover, ~60s RTO)
ElastiCache promoted to Multi-AZ (cache.t4g.small) with auto-failover
Second NAT Gateway added (one per AZ) — eliminates AZ-single-point for outbound egress
EventBridge pipeline bus + SQS render queue replace direct Lambda invoke — decouples orchestrator from renderer, absorbs burst, adds DLQ
Render Lambda now SQS-triggered (event source mapping, idempotent, DLQ-backed)
Cross-region S3 replication (CRR) for backup bucket — DR-grade backup copy in secondary region
CloudWatch Anomaly Detection + SLO alarms + PagerDuty escalation topic for critical paths
Burst handling: SQS buffers render spike; Lambda reserved concurrency controls drain rate; Fargate auto-scales across 2 AZs
tradeoffs: Highest cost tier: second NAT, Multi-AZ RDS, Multi-AZ ElastiCache, EventBridge + SQS, CRR
Multi-AZ RDS is standby (not read replica) — read scale still needs explicit read-replica promotion if needed
SQS async render path adds latency vs. synchronous invoke; acceptable for pipeline (not user-request) path
Cross-region is backup CRR only — active-active multi-region not included (deferred; would require Aurora Global / DynamoDB Global Tables)

keyDecisions.decision: Budget compute shape: single box vs. managed split
keyDecisions.chosen: Single EC2 t4g.medium (Docker Compose: Next.js web + orchestrator + self-managed Postgres/PostGIS) + render Lambda
alternativesConsidered: Fargate + RDS + ALB + NAT (balanced-tier managed split)
keyDecisions.rationale: Cost Optimization: a single host with self-managed Postgres matches the current footprint at ~$0 incremental infra cost vs. the ~$100+/mo always-on NAT+ALB+RDS quartet that belongs at balanced; render Lambda split out first per the brief's stated goal.

keyDecisions.decision: Render workload isolation: Lambda vs. Fargate spot task
keyDecisions.chosen: Lambda (2048 MB, arm64) with S3 large-output offload
alternativesConsidered: Fargate Spot task triggered via ECS RunTask
keyDecisions.rationale: Performance Efficiency + Cost Optimization: scale-to-zero pay-per-invocation perfectly matches the spiky/idle headless-Chromium pattern; S3 offload solves the >6 MB payload constraint without a custom HTTP proxy.

keyDecisions.decision: Postgres/PostGIS at budget: self-managed on EC2 vs. RDS
keyDecisions.chosen: Self-managed Postgres + PostGIS extension on the single EC2 box (localhost-bound, nightly pg_dump → S3)
alternativesConsidered: RDS for PostgreSQL with PostGIS (rds.force_ssl, multi-AZ option)
keyDecisions.rationale: Cost Optimization: PostGIS is a standard extension installable on any Postgres; a separate RDS instance adds ~$30–50/mo always-on cost with no robustness benefit at single-AZ scale — promoted to balanced where multi-AZ and connection pooling justify it.

keyDecisions.decision: Balanced tier: promote to managed split (Fargate + RDS + ALB + NAT)
keyDecisions.chosen: Fargate (web + orchestrator as separate services) + RDS for PostgreSQL with PostGIS + ALB + NAT Gateway + ElastiCache Redis (ISR session cache)
keyDecisions.rationale: Reliability + Operational Excellence: separating tiers onto managed services enables independent scaling, automated patching, and multi-AZ promotion in the resilient tier without redesign; NAT+ALB cost is the explicit trade-off vs. budget.

keyDecisions.decision: Job queue mechanism: Postgres-backed vs. SQS
keyDecisions.chosen: Postgres-backed queue (pg-boss) at budget/balanced; EventBridge + SQS fan-out added at resilient
keyDecisions.rationale: Cost Optimization + Reliability: Postgres is already present; pg-boss gives SKIP LOCKED job semantics, retries, and visibility — no extra service or cost at budget/balanced; SQS fan-out promoted at resilient where multi-consumer pipeline branches justify it.

keyDecisions.chosen: File-system ISR cache on the container's ephemeral storage (Next.js default); S3 as the revalidation write-through backing store at balanced+
alternativesConsidered: ElastiCache Redis as ISR cache from the start
keyDecisions.rationale: Cost Optimization: in-process/file-system cache has zero marginal cost at budget; S3-backed ISR write-through + CloudFront CDN promotion at balanced gives durable cross-task cache consistency when multiple Fargate tasks run.

securityFloor: Encrypt all data at rest (KMS / service-native SSE), preferring customer-managed keys.
Enforce TLS in transit everywhere; terminate HTTPS at the edge and reject plaintext.
Least-privilege IAM: scoped per-service roles, no wildcards, no long-lived access keys.
Enable S3 Block Public Access account-wide so no bucket or object can be made public.
Keep the data tier private: VPC-bound stores in private subnets with no public route; managed/serverless stores (DynamoDB/S3) are already private via IAM and need no VPC or NAT.
Store secrets in AWS Secrets Manager / SSM Parameter Store — never in code or images.
Front public endpoints with CloudFront + AWS WAF (managed + rate-based rules); Shield Standard for L3/L4.
Enable CloudTrail plus S3/CloudFront/ALB access logs and VPC Flow Logs for audit.
